title: First Driving Lesson
summary: A driving instructor guides a nervous learner through her very first lesson — adjusting the seat, checking mirrors, and carefully pulling out of a car park for the first time.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Good morning, Claire. Welcome to your first lesson. How are you feeling?

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Nervous, honestly. I've never actually sat in the driver's seat before.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: That's completely normal. Most people feel exactly the same on their first lesson. We'll take it very slowly, and I want you to know that I have dual controls on my side, so I can brake or steer at any time if I need to. You're completely safe.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: That's reassuring. Thank you.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Right. Before we do anything else, let's make sure you're comfortable. Adjust your seat so that your foot can press the clutch pedal all the way down without fully stretching your leg. Give it a try.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Like this? I think I need to move it forward a little.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Exactly right. Pull the lever underneath — there you go. That looks much better. Now adjust the steering wheel height so it's not in the way of your view of the dashboard.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Okay, done. This feels more natural now.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Good. Now mirrors. This is really important. Before you ever move the car, you need to know exactly what's around you. Start with the interior mirror — that one in the middle.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: I can see the back window through it now.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Perfect. Now the left wing mirror — you want to see a thin strip of the car on one side and the full view of the road behind.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Okay. And the right one as well?

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Yes, the same on the right. Good. Now we're going to talk through the cockpit drill. That means checking everything inside the car before moving. Doors closed?

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Yes, mine is.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Seatbelt on?

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Yes.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Good. Now, when you're ready, put your left foot on the clutch and press it all the way down. Then start the engine with the key.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Okay... it's on. The car is running. That's a strange feeling.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: You'll get used to it quickly. Now, we're going to pull out of this car park very slowly. I want you to find the biting point — that's the point where the clutch begins to engage the engine. You'll feel the car shudder slightly. Can you find it?

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: I think so... yes! I can feel it. It's trembling a little.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: That's it. Now check your mirrors again — interior, right, left — then look over your right shoulder for a blind spot check. Take your time.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: All clear. No other cars nearby.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: Excellent. Now very gently release the handbrake with your right hand and let the clutch up just a little bit more. Don't rush it.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: We're moving! Oh — that surprised me.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: That's great! You're doing really well. Keep your speed very low. This car park is perfect for practising because there's nobody else here. Just let the car roll forward slowly. Steer very gently if you need to.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: I'm steering! This is incredible. It's much harder to steer than I expected.

body.speaker: Instructor
body.text: That's because the car is moving slowly. Steering is lighter at higher speeds, but we're not going there today. You're doing brilliantly for a first lesson, Claire. Really.

body.speaker: Learner
body.text: Thank you. I still feel nervous, but it's getting easier every minute.

annotations.meaningVi: hệ thống điều khiển kép (giáo viên cũng có thể kiểm soát xe)
annotations.exampleEn: Driving school cars have dual controls for safety.
Press the clutch pedal before changing gear.
Keep both hands on the steering wheel at all times.
Check the interior mirror before changing lanes.
The wing mirror was knocked off in the accident.

annotations.meaningVi: quy trình kiểm tra trước khi lái xe (DSSSM)
annotations.exampleEn: Always do a cockpit drill before you start the engine.

annotations.meaningVi: điểm cắn (điểm ly hợp bắt đầu kết nối động cơ)
annotations.exampleEn: Finding the biting point is one of the hardest skills for new drivers.

annotations.meaningVi: kết nối với động cơ, truyền lực từ động cơ
annotations.exampleEn: The clutch engages the engine when released slowly.
The old engine shuddered when it started up.

annotations.meaningVi: điểm mù (vùng không nhìn thấy trong gương)
annotations.exampleEn: Always check your blind spot before changing lanes.
Release the handbrake gently when moving uphill.

annotations.meaningVi: nhả từ từ bàn đạp ly hợp
annotations.exampleEn: Let the clutch up slowly to avoid stalling.
Let the car roll forward slowly before accelerating.
You're doing brilliantly — keep it up!
The doctor's calm manner was very reassuring.
Take your time — there's no rush.

grammarNotes.title: Imperatives and Modal Verbs for Instructions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại sử dụng nhiều câu mệnh lệnh (imperative) để đưa ra hướng dẫn trực tiếp: 'Adjust your seat', 'Press the clutch', 'Check your mirrors'. Đồng thời dùng các động từ khuyết thiếu (modal verbs) để diễn đạt sự cần thiết ('you need to know') và khả năng ('I can brake'). Trong bối cảnh hướng dẫn, sự kết hợp giữa imperative và modal tạo ra giọng điệu vừa rõ ràng vừa hỗ trợ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses imperatives for direct instructions: 'Adjust your seat', 'Press the clutch', 'Check your mirrors'. Modal verbs express necessity ('you need to know') and ability ('I can brake'). In instructional contexts, combining imperatives with modals creates a tone that is clear and supportive.

grammarNotes.title: Present Continuous for In-Progress Actions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi xe bắt đầu chuyển động, learner dùng thì hiện tại tiếp diễn để mô tả những gì đang xảy ra ngay lúc đó: 'We're moving!', 'I'm steering!'. Thì này thể hiện hành động đang diễn ra ở thời điểm nói. So sánh với thì hiện tại đơn ('It's getting easier every minute') dùng để mô tả quá trình thay đổi dần dần.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: As the car begins moving, the learner uses the present continuous to describe actions happening at that exact moment: 'We're moving!', 'I'm steering!' This tense captures an action in progress at the moment of speaking. Compare with the present simple used for gradual change: 'it's getting easier every minute'.

translationVi:
Instructor: Chào buổi sáng, Claire. Chào mừng đến với buổi học đầu tiên. Cô cảm thấy thế nào?
Learner: Thành thật mà nói, tôi rất hồi hộp. Trước giờ tôi chưa bao giờ thực sự ngồi vào ghế lái xe.
Instructor: Điều đó hoàn toàn bình thường. Hầu hết mọi người đều cảm thấy như vậy trong buổi học đầu tiên. Chúng ta sẽ đi rất chậm, và tôi muốn cô biết rằng tôi có hệ thống điều khiển kép ở phía mình, vì vậy tôi có thể phanh hoặc lái bất cứ lúc nào nếu cần. Cô hoàn toàn an toàn.
Learner: Điều đó thật trấn an. Cảm ơn anh.
Instructor: Được rồi. Trước khi làm bất cứ điều gì khác, hãy đảm bảo cô ngồi thoải mái. Điều chỉnh ghế sao cho chân cô có thể nhấn bàn đạp ly hợp xuống hết mà không phải duỗi chân hoàn toàn. Thử xem nào.
Learner: Như thế này? Tôi nghĩ tôi cần dịch ghế ra phía trước một chút.
Instructor: Đúng vậy. Kéo cần gạt bên dưới — đó rồi. Trông khá hơn nhiều rồi. Bây giờ điều chỉnh chiều cao vô lăng sao cho nó không cản tầm nhìn của cô vào bảng điều khiển.
Learner: Được rồi, xong. Cảm giác tự nhiên hơn nhiều rồi.
Instructor: Tốt. Bây giờ đến gương. Điều này thực sự quan trọng. Trước khi di chuyển xe, cô cần biết chính xác những gì xung quanh mình. Bắt đầu với gương chiếu hậu trong xe — cái ở giữa đó.
Learner: Bây giờ tôi có thể nhìn thấy cửa sổ phía sau qua gương rồi.
Instructor: Hoàn hảo. Bây giờ gương cánh trái — cô muốn thấy một dải mỏng của xe ở một bên và toàn bộ tầm nhìn của đường phía sau.
Learner: Được rồi. Và gương bên phải cũng vậy?
Instructor: Đúng vậy, giống nhau bên phải. Tốt. Bây giờ chúng ta sẽ nói qua quy trình kiểm tra trước khi lái. Điều đó có nghĩa là kiểm tra mọi thứ bên trong xe trước khi di chuyển. Cửa đã đóng chưa?
Learner: Rồi, cửa của tôi đóng rồi.
Instructor: Dây an toàn đã thắt chưa?
Learner: Rồi ạ.
Instructor: Tốt. Bây giờ, khi cô sẵn sàng, đặt chân trái lên bàn đạp ly hợp và nhấn xuống hết. Sau đó khởi động động cơ bằng chìa khóa.
Learner: Được rồi... động cơ bật rồi. Xe đang chạy. Cảm giác kỳ lạ thật.
Instructor: Cô sẽ quen ngay thôi. Bây giờ, chúng ta sẽ lùi ra khỏi bãi đỗ xe này rất chậm. Tôi muốn cô tìm điểm cắn — đó là điểm ly hợp bắt đầu kết nối với động cơ. Cô sẽ cảm thấy xe rung nhẹ. Cô có tìm được không?
Learner: Tôi nghĩ là có... rồi! Tôi cảm thấy nó rồi. Nó đang run nhẹ.
Instructor: Đúng rồi. Bây giờ kiểm tra gương một lần nữa — gương trong, bên phải, bên trái — rồi nhìn qua vai phải để kiểm tra điểm mù. Đừng vội.
Learner: Đường thông. Không có xe nào gần đây.
Instructor: Tuyệt vời. Bây giờ rất nhẹ nhàng nhả phanh tay bằng tay phải và tiếp tục nhả bàn đạp ly hợp lên một chút nữa. Đừng vội.
Learner: Xe đang di chuyển! Ồ — điều đó làm tôi giật mình.
Instructor: Tuyệt lắm! Cô đang làm rất tốt. Giữ tốc độ rất thấp. Bãi đỗ xe này hoàn hảo để luyện tập vì không có ai khác ở đây. Chỉ cần để xe lăn chậm về phía trước. Lái nhẹ nhàng nếu cần.
Learner: Tôi đang lái rồi! Không thể tin được. Lái khó hơn tôi tưởng nhiều.
Instructor: Vì xe đang chạy chậm. Lái nhẹ hơn khi tốc độ cao hơn, nhưng hôm nay chúng ta chưa đến đó. Cô đang làm xuất sắc cho buổi học đầu tiên, Claire. Thực sự đó.
Learner: Cảm ơn anh. Tôi vẫn còn hồi hộp, nhưng mọi thứ đang dễ dàng hơn từng phút.

questions.prompt: Why does the instructor say Claire is completely safe?
options: The car park is empty so there is no risk
The instructor has dual controls and can brake or steer at any time
The car has automatic emergency braking
Claire has already practised in a simulator
questions.explanation: The instructor says 'I have dual controls on my side, so I can brake or steer at any time if I need to.'
questions.hint: The instructor explains this right after Claire says she is nervous.

questions.prompt: Why does the instructor ask Claire to adjust her seat?
options: So she can see over the steering wheel clearly
So her foot can press the clutch pedal all the way down without fully stretching her leg
So she is closer to the handbrake
questions.explanation: The instructor says: 'Adjust your seat so that your foot can press the clutch pedal all the way down without fully stretching your leg.'
questions.hint: Think about what the instructor says the correct seat position should allow.

questions.prompt: What should the wing mirror show, according to the instructor?
options: Only the road behind the car
A thin strip of the car on one side and the full view of the road behind
The full side of the car and a narrow strip of road
The area directly behind the car bumper
questions.explanation: The instructor says: 'you want to see a thin strip of the car on one side and the full view of the road behind.'
questions.hint: The instructor describes the correct mirror alignment carefully.

options: A series of steering exercises in a car park
A checklist for adjusting mirrors and seat
Checking everything inside the car before moving
The process of starting the engine for the first time
questions.explanation: The instructor explains: 'That means checking everything inside the car before moving.'
questions.hint: The instructor defines it immediately after introducing the term.

questions.prompt: How does a driver find the 'biting point'?
options: By pressing the accelerator until the engine revs loudly
By slowly releasing the clutch until the car begins to shudder slightly
By pressing the brake and clutch simultaneously
By starting the engine and immediately releasing the handbrake
questions.explanation: The instructor explains it is 'the point where the clutch begins to engage the engine. You'll feel the car shudder slightly.'
questions.hint: The biting point is about what you feel, not what you hear.

questions.prompt: In what order does the instructor tell Claire to check her mirrors before moving?
questions.explanation: The instructor says: 'check your mirrors again — interior, right, left — then look over your right shoulder for a blind spot check.'
questions.hint: The instructor specifies the order when asking Claire to check before moving.

questions.prompt: What does Claire do after checking her mirrors before moving?
options: She looks over her right shoulder for a blind spot check
She puts the car in first gear
She signals to indicate she is pulling out
questions.explanation: The instructor tells her: 'then look over your right shoulder for a blind spot check.'
questions.hint: The mirror check has one final step before releasing the handbrake.

questions.prompt: Why does Claire find steering harder than expected?
options: The steering wheel is too stiff and needs adjustment
She is sitting too far from the wheel
The car is moving slowly, and steering is heavier at low speeds
She is gripping the wheel too tightly
questions.explanation: The instructor explains: 'Steering is lighter at higher speeds, but we're not going there today.'
questions.hint: The instructor directly explains why steering feels heavy at their current speed.

questions.prompt: Why does the instructor say the car park is a perfect place to practise?
options: It has markings that help learners practise parking
It is flat and there are no hills to manage
It is close to the main road so Claire can practise joining traffic
questions.explanation: The instructor says: 'This car park is perfect for practising because there's nobody else here.'
questions.hint: The instructor gives the reason directly.

questions.prompt: How does Claire feel at the end of the dialogue?
options: Still very nervous and not ready to continue
Confident and ready to drive on a public road
Still nervous but finding it easier every minute
Frustrated because the car is difficult to control
questions.explanation: Claire says: 'I still feel nervous, but it's getting easier every minute.'
questions.hint: Claire gives two pieces of information about her feelings at the end.

cloze.template:
Instructor: Good morning, Claire. Welcome to your first lesson. How are you feeling?
Learner: Nervous, honestly. I've never actually sat in the driver's seat before.
Instructor: That's completely normal. Most people feel exactly the same on their first lesson. We'll take it very slowly, and I want you to know that I have {{b1}} on my side, so I can brake or steer at any time if I need to. You're completely safe.
Learner: That's {{b2}}. Thank you.
Instructor: Right. Before we do anything else, let's make sure you're comfortable. Adjust your seat so that your foot can press the {{b3}} all the way down without fully stretching your leg. Give it a try.
Learner: Like this? I think I need to move it forward a little.
Instructor: Exactly right. Pull the lever underneath — there you go. That looks much better. Now adjust the steering wheel height so it's not in the way of your view of the dashboard.
Learner: Okay, done. This feels more natural now.
Instructor: Good. Now mirrors. This is really important. Before you ever move the car, you need to know exactly what's around you. Start with the interior mirror — that one in the middle.
Learner: I can see the back window through it now.
Instructor: Perfect. Now the left wing mirror — you want to see a thin strip of the car on one side and the full view of the road behind.
Learner: Okay. And the right one as well?
Instructor: Yes, the same on the right. Good. Now we're going to talk through the {{b4}}. That means checking everything inside the car before moving. Doors closed?
Learner: Yes, mine is.
Instructor: Seatbelt on?
Learner: Yes.
Instructor: Good. Now, when you're ready, put your left foot on the clutch and press it all the way down. Then start the engine with the key.
Learner: Okay... it's on. The car is running. That's a strange feeling.
Instructor: You'll get used to it quickly. Now, we're going to pull out of this car park very slowly. I want you to find the {{b5}} — that's the point where the clutch begins to {{b6}} the engine. You'll feel the car shudder slightly. Can you find it?
Learner: I think so... yes! I can feel it. It's trembling a little.
Instructor: That's it. Now check your mirrors again — interior, right, left — then look over your right shoulder for a {{b7}} check. Take your time.
Learner: All clear. No other cars nearby.
Instructor: Excellent. Now very gently release the {{b8}} with your right hand and let the clutch up just a little bit more. Don't rush it.
Learner: We're moving! Oh — that surprised me.
Instructor: That's great! You're doing really well. Keep your speed very low. This car park is perfect for practising because there's nobody else here. Just let the car {{b9}} forward slowly. Steer very gently if you need to.
Learner: I'm steering! This is incredible. It's much harder to steer than I expected.
Instructor: That's because the car is moving slowly. Steering is lighter at higher speeds, but we're not going there today. You're doing {{b10}} for a first lesson, Claire. Really.
Learner: Thank you. I still feel nervous, but it's getting easier every minute.

blanks.explanation: 'Dual controls' means the instructor's side of the car also has a clutch and brake, allowing them to control the car if necessary.
'Reassuring' means making someone feel less worried or afraid — Claire uses it to say the information makes her feel safer.
The instructor refers to the 'clutch pedal' — the pedal used to disengage and engage the gears in a manual car.
The 'cockpit drill' is a standard UK driving lesson term for the pre-drive check of all controls and settings inside the car.
The 'biting point' is the precise position of the clutch pedal where the engine and gearbox begin to connect, making the car want to move.
'Engage the engine' is the mechanical term used — it describes the clutch mechanically linking with the engine.
A 'blind spot' is an area around the vehicle that cannot be seen in the mirrors — hence the over-the-shoulder check.
The 'handbrake' (also called the parking brake or emergency brake) is operated by hand and keeps the car stationary.
'Roll forward' means to move gently forward without pressing the accelerator — the car moves under its own momentum.
'Doing brilliantly' is a British English expression meaning doing extremely well — a strong word of encouragement.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Learning to drive is one of the most anxiety-inducing experiences for many adults, yet it is also one of the most empowering. What makes learning a complex physical skill so emotionally challenging? How does having a patient and structured teacher change the experience — and what can this tell us about how people learn best in any context?
